serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate serving of Dum Aloo with Jeera Rice is approximately 350 g, comprising roughly 200 g of jeera rice and 150 g of dum aloo curry, as typically served at a Singaporean Indian hawker stall or restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: Dum Aloo with Jeera Rice — HPB Singapore Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Dum Aloo with Jeera Rice (fat-free variant), cr_id G00010524. All per-100g macros and micronutrients are taken verbatim from the HPB record. Saturated fat is not separately reported in the HPB entry; estimated at 0.8 g/100g based on ICMR-NIN data for comparable low-fat potato curry preparations.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; University of Sydney GI Database — Basmati rice and potato curry analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Dum Aloo with Jeera Rice (fat-free variant) in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 56 (medium) based on a weighted composite: basmati rice GI ~58 (University of Sydney GI Database, Atkinson et al. 2021) contributing approximately 60% of carbohydrate load, and boiled potato GI ~78 (University of Sydney GI Database) contributing approximately 40% of carbohydrate load. Yogurt, fibre (3.7 g/100g), and spice matrix (turmeric, ginger) are expected to moderately attenuate the composite GI. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (56 × 88.2) / 100 ≈ 49.4, where carbohydrate per 350 g serving = 25.19 × 3.5 = 88.2 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 231 mg/100g partially offset by potassium 327 mg/100g (Na:K ratio favourable at ~0.71); however, cumulative sodium per full plate serving (~809 mg) approaches 35% of the 2300 mg daily limit.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension may consume this dish in a controlled portion (150–200 g) and should request reduced salt in the masala and minimal or no ghee in the rice to keep per-meal sodium below 500 mg; the favourable potassium-to-sodium ratio and 3.7 g/100g dietary fibre support DASH-diet compatibility at moderate portions.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Dual starch load (basmati rice GI ~58 + boiled potato GI ~78) produces a high glycaemic load per standard serving (~49 GL units); carbohydrate density is 25.19 g/100g with limited protein buffering at 6.5 g/100g.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should limit this dish to a maximum 150 g portion, substitute cauliflower rice for basmati rice where possible, pair with a high-protein side to blunt the glycaemic response, and monitor post-prandial glucose at 1 and 2 hours given the high composite glycaemic load from the dual starch matrix.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Cholesterol is 0 mg/100g; fat is 4.21 g/100g with estimated saturated fat ~0.8 g/100g; dietary fibre at 3.7 g/100g supports LDL reduction; potassium at 327 mg/100g supports vascular health.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: This fat-free variant of Dum Aloo with Jeera Rice is broadly compatible with a heart-healthy diet given its negligible cholesterol, low saturated fat, and meaningful dietary fibre; patients with cardiovascular disease should nonetheless monitor portion size to manage total carbohydrate and sodium intake per meal.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Multiple nightshade ingredients (potato, tomato, red/kashmiri chili) contain solanine alkaloids that may aggravate joint inflammation in nightshade-sensitive RA patients.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis and known nightshade sensitivity should avoid this dish due to the combined presence of potato, tomato, and chili; turmeric and ginger provide partial anti-inflammatory benefit but do not offset the nightshade alkaloid burden in sensitive individuals.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Dum Aloo with Jeera Rice (Fat-Free Variant) is a North Indian vegetarian dish popular in Singapore's Indian hawker and restaurant scene. 'Dum Aloo' refers to baby potatoes slow-cooked (dum method) in a spiced masala sauce made from tomato, onion, yogurt, ginger, garlic, turmeric, kashmiri red chili, coriander powder, and garam masala. The 'free' or fat-free designation indicates that ghee or oil is minimised or eliminated in the preparation, reducing the fat content compared to the standard variant. 'Jeera Rice' is basmati rice tempered with cumin seeds (jeera), producing a fragrant, lightly spiced rice that complements the rich potato curry. The dish has a warm, earthy, mildly spiced flavour profile with a soft, creamy potato texture and fluffy, aromatic rice. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides 164 kcal, 6.5 g protein, 4.21 g fat, 25.19 g carbohydrate, 3.7 g dietary fibre, 231 mg sodium, and 327 mg potassium. The high dietary fibre and favourable potassium-to-sodium ratio make it moderately suitable for hypertension management, while the dual starch load (potato + basmati rice) creates a high glycaemic load that requires careful portion control for patients with Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is vegetarian, halal, gluten-free, and dairy-containing (yogurt). It is commonly served as a lunch or dinner main course in Singapore.
